title: Karl Marx in Kalbadevi — Revolution via Entertainment
excerpt: A fine example of revolution through entertainment. You just can't keep your eyes off Satchit Puranik while he enacts different emotions with remarkable ease. The play deserves to be translated to other languages.

title: Karl Marx In Kalbadevi — This Karlbhai Was One Clever Dude
excerpt: Manoj Shah embarked on an audacious production to deliver a sermon on socialism to incontrovertible capitalists with the mandate to make it entertaining. Enough to provoke the audience to go back thinking.

title: Karl Marx In Kalbadevi — Review
excerpt: A title like Karl Marx In Kalbadevi is enticing, full of prospects. Satchit Puranik ably enacts this one-person play at breakneck tempo. I would dare these mavericks to be more risky and provoking.

title: Existential Parables and Tortured Souls
excerpt: Shah's latest play Kaagdo draws from the essences of Japanese ikigai, the mysticism of Sufism, and the tenets of Zen Buddhism to mount an existential parable that is not didactic in its approach. 'Even sadness is a tradition of happiness,' says Shah.

title: Jal Jal Mare Patang — Gujarati Drama Review
excerpt: Manoj Shah's biographical play on Manilal Dwivedi, the 19th-century Gujarati philosopher who introduced Western philosophy to Gujarat, features backdrop paintings by Atul Dodiya.

title: A Play Featuring Chandrakant Bakshi's Life Opens on 15 June
excerpt: Ideas Unlimited, Manoj Shah's theatre company, will stage a biographical play on controversial Gujarati writer Chandrakant Bakshi, starring Pratik Gandhi, at Prithvi Theatre on 15 June.

title: Hun Chandrakant Bakshi: A Riveting Drama on the Audacious Gujarati Author
excerpt: Pratik Gandhi brought the bold and egotist protagonist author to life as he essayed the role to flawless perfection — cautious yet fearless.

title: A Play to Celebrate Life and Times of India's First Female Doctor
excerpt: Written by Geeta Manek and directed by Manoj Shah, the solo play performed by Manasi Joshi uses a social media conceit to draw parallels between 19th-century struggles and modern Indian women's issues.

title: Dr. Anandibai: Like, Comment, Share — NCPA CentreStage
excerpt: Actress Manasi Joshi describes the struggles and sacrifices of Dr Anandibai Joshi, who died at age 22, in this Gujarati play selected for the Theatre Olympics.

title: Saptapadi Stuns Audiences at Prithvi Theatre
excerpt: Manoj Shah's latest directorial venture is a tour de force of emotional storytelling, with Neha Joshi delivering a career-best performance.

title: A Definitive Adhe Adhure for Our Times
excerpt: Ideas Unlimited's revival of Mohan Rakesh's classic feels startlingly contemporary, anchored by powerhouse performances.

title: Kaumudi: A Love Letter to Gujarati Theatre
excerpt: Manoj Shah returns to his Gujarati roots with a sweeping historical epic that rivals anything on the Hindi stage.

title: 35 Years of Fearless Theatre: The Ideas Unlimited Story
excerpt: From a small experimental group to one of India's most respected theatre companies, the journey of Ideas Unlimited Productions is a testament to artistic conviction.

title: Manoj Shah: The Quiet Revolutionary of Indian Theatre
excerpt: In a rare interview, the founder of Ideas Unlimited Productions reflects on three decades of pushing boundaries.

title: The Poet in the Bedroom
excerpt: Here's an alternative to oh-so-popular Gujarati bedroom comedies: Manoj Shah, the theatre director from Prithvi. He likes mixing up and meshing diverse styles. His guiding principles: Lage raho, lage raho.

title: Kathakaar of Gujarat: Manoj Shah, the Man of Theatre
excerpt: His salt and pepper beard and hair, eyes peaking through glasses, and a mischievous sense of adventure. He has worked in every capacity — stage extra, actor, director, visualizer, and conceptualizer.

title: Manoj Shah Interview: Three-Day Festival at Prithvi Theatre
excerpt: The plays are a reflection of my work in the theatre since the last eleven years. A gentleman has seen all the shows of Mareez in Mumbai.

title: Manoj Shah on Adapting Ibsen for Gujarati Theatre
excerpt: It's a physiological thriller, part tragedy, part human drama. He incorporated Hindu temple architecture and the Sompura community instead of Gothic architecture.

title: Tagore ni Rachana nu Gujarati Connection
excerpt: The only Gujarati play presented at the five-day Tagore Festival in Kolkata — Manoj Shah's Achalayatan explores the rigidity pervading religion and education through Tagore's most allegorical work. Shah describes the play's tone as one of self-discovery rather than opposition to systems.

title: Adbhut: A Gujarati Play That Makes You Feel Again
excerpt: RJ Devaki's solo performance dissolves the boundary between stage and audience, turning strangers into co-performers in an interactive exploration of mental health that leaves no one untouched.

title: Blast From The Past: New Hindi Play Revolves Around Bhamasha
excerpt: Whether Shah Jahan, Ashoka, or Bhamasha — Indian history is full of intriguing tales. Veteran actor Dayashankar Pandey brings the 16th-century Jain warrior to life, with folk musician Chugge Khan and his Rajasthani ensemble providing the score.

title: History Comes Alive On Stage
excerpt: This Republic Day, catch a Hindi play that joys tribute to Bhamasha, advisor to Maharana Pratap who donated all his wealth to Mewar to aid the war against the Mughals.

title: A Colorful, Wilting Bloom
excerpt: The 120-minute staging brings out the highs and lows of Ruttie's life, her love for and obsession with Jinnah, her later dependence on drink and opium, her solitary wanderings in Europe and her loneliness. Bhamini Oza Gandhi shines as Ruttie.

title: 41 Varsh ni Vay na Mohammed Ali Jinnah e Matra 18 Varsh ni Ruttie Sathe Lagna Karya
excerpt: A detailed account of the Ruttie Petit-Jinnah story and Ideas Unlimited's Bombay Flower — the Gujarati play that brings this forgotten chapter of pre-Partition history to the stage.

title: A Play on Jinnah's Wife and South Bombay's Pride Ruttie
excerpt: We were looking at doing a one-woman play and Ruttie was interesting. When we started working on it, something was amiss, so we brought in Jinnah.

title: Why Mughal-e-Azam: The Musical Matters
excerpt: The ambitious musical adaptation proves that Indian theatre can match Broadway in scale and spectacle.
